title: Thierry Baudet: the Dutch nationalist out to take Europe by storm
text: As citizens across Europe head to the polls today, pundits are predicting a surge in support for the Continent's myriad populist parties as voters turn their backs on the Establishment. But few populist politicians have seen their support base swell so much as that of Thierry Baudet, the founder and leader of the Forum for Democracy (FvD), a controversial far-right party in the Netherlands that was formed just three years ago. Described by The Times as a populist upstart, the 36-year-old's outspoken views have seen the FvD poll neck-and-neck with Dutch President Mark Rutte's ruling liberal People's Party for Freedom and Democracy (VVD). The Guardian describes him as a philosopher-dandy with a penchant for playing piano in his office, posing nude on social media and quoting Latin in parliament. Left-leaning US news site Jacobin says the FvD is the most self-consciously elitist party in the Dutch political landscape. The FvD candidate list is made up of lawyers, surgeons, corporate managers, businessmen, retired military officers, and the party's programmatic statements promise class war from above on all fronts, the site adds. The New Statesman notes that the Dutch have experienced the same universal factors that tend to foster populism across the Western world, including deindustrialisation, a neoliberal consensus, social fragmentation and migration. Born in the Dutch town of Heemstede in 1983, Baudet studied law before working as a columnist for NRC Handelsblad, a liberal newspaper based in Amsterdam. His politics later veered to the extreme nationalist right, and he published books on anti-immigration and Eurosceptic themes. Europe was stunned when Baudet's FvD became the largest party in the Dutch senate at the provincial elections in March, ahead of the European elections in May. Latest polls suggested Baudet's party and Rutte's ruling VVD were level pegging, with both claiming around 15 percent of the vote share, with the Dutch Labour party (PvdA), GreenLeft and Christian Democratic Appeal following close behind at around 13 percent each. According to Politico, opponents of Baudet see him as a pseudo-intellectual whose sophisticated air masks extreme-right views. GreenLeft leader Jesse Klaver argued that Baudet fosters a sad old-fashioned view of men and women. University professor Amy Verdun believes that most voters will ultimately back centrist parties, due to the repeated failure of squabbling populist and far-right parties to unite within the European Parliament.

title: Macron's reckoning: The European Parliament elections in France
text: The European Parliament elections will be the first major test at the polls for Emmanuel Macron since being elected president in 2017. These elections come after six months of street protests that have derailed a reform programme the president is now anxious to get back on track. The elections are set to be a tightly fought battle for supremacy between Macron's centrist, liberal, pro-EU governing party LREM and Marine Le Pen's nationalist, populist, anti-EU party RN. Currently, the LREM and RN lists were neck-and-neck at around 22 percent of vote projections, way out in front of all others, with the RN nudging ahead in some recent polls. Since November 2018, Macron found his reform agenda derailed by a popular insurgency, the gilets jaunes protests, accompanied by the worst civil disorder seen in France for decades. The protests began against a planned fuel tax rise and grew into a wider movement against the political class. Macron conceded around 10 billion euros in pay rises and tax exemptions in December, followed by close to 10 billion euros more in tax cuts and pension rises in April, at the end of a great national debate that saw over 10,000 town hall meetings between January and March. Macron's approval ratings sank to the mid-20s during this period. Macron must ensure his Renaissance list finishes ahead of Le Pen's list to signal his recovery from the protests and avoid what is seen by many as a referendum on his presidency. Le Pen, for her part, needed her party's list to finish ahead of Macron's to match its 2014 European election performance of 24.9 percent and 24 seats. The centre-right Republicans occupied third place in projections at around 14 percent, with Jean-Luc Melenchon's far-left France Unbowed on 8 to 9 percent and the Greens on 7 to 8 percent. France's Socialists, in power under Francois Hollande from 2012 to 2017, were polling at barely 5 percent. Projected turnout was around 40 percent, which would break the record for these traditionally low-turnout elections in France. In recent polling, only 29 percent of French respondents saw the European project as a source of hope, down from 61 percent in 2003.

title: France's far-right tops European election, narrow loss for Macron
text: Marine Le Pen's eurosceptic National Rally narrowly topped the European Parliament vote in France on the night of the election, based on exit polls and early results, edging out President Emmanuel Macron's centrist alliance by a small margin. Macron's office described the outcome as disappointing but not disastrous, noting his party's vote share was lower than his 2017 presidential first-round result. The French Greens placed third in a crowded field, a result that, combined with a similarly strong showing from Germany's Greens, was seen as evidence of a possible green wave that could shape EU policy in the coming years. The result came at a difficult moment for Macron, whose economic reform agenda had been disrupted by months of gilets jaunes protests and historic lows in his approval ratings, though analysts said the narrow loss to Le Pen was unlikely to derail his domestic plans, including a planned pension overhaul. This was the first European election fought by Macron's party, which he founded ahead of the 2017 presidential race; Le Pen's party, by contrast, had topped the previous European election in 2014 with a larger vote share than it achieved this time. Early Parliament-wide projections suggested the chamber's traditional centrist bloc would not secure a majority, with strong performances from liberal and green parties in Germany and France alongside eurosceptic gains in Italy, Hungary and the UK pointing to a more fragmented Parliament over the next five years. Turnout across the EU was estimated between 49 and 51 percent, up from 43 percent in 2014, with France recording its highest turnout in decades at around 50 percent.

title: Dutch Labour wins surprise victory in Netherlands EU vote
text: The Dutch Labour party emerged as the unexpected winner of the European Parliament election in the Netherlands, scoring just over 18 percent of the vote according to exit polls, ahead of the conservative VVD party of Prime Minister Mark Rutte, which placed second at around 15 percent. Thierry Baudet's eurosceptic Forum for Democracy, which had been polling neck-and-neck with the VVD in the days before the vote, instead finished third at around 11 percent. The result was a significant surprise, since pre-election polling had consistently placed Labour in third place at best, with Forum for Democracy and the VVD seen as the front-runners. The win was expected to boost the prospects of Labour's lead candidate, Frans Timmermans, in his bid for the European Commission presidency; Timmermans said the result could provide momentum for social democrats elsewhere in Europe. The Netherlands voted on the same day as the United Kingdom, the first two EU countries to go to the polls in the European election cycle, and the result was described as a relief for pro-EU parties elsewhere in the bloc following weeks of polling that had suggested strong gains for eurosceptic and populist parties across Europe. Far-right parties were still expected to increase their overall number of seats in the new European Parliament, though analysts did not expect them to win more than roughly a fifth of the total seats.

title: EU election forecast: Le Pen edges Macron, Dutch eurosceptics gain ground
text: Pre-election projections from the polling analysis group Europe Elects showed France's eurosceptic National Rally, led by Marine Le Pen, running neck-and-neck with President Emmanuel Macron's centrist alliance, with the two blocs essentially tied. Europe Elects cautioned against reading this as evidence of rising momentum for Le Pen's party, noting that her party's projected vote share was largely unchanged while Macron's had declined, with smaller parties picking up the difference. The group's founder noted that Le Pen's party had won 25 percent of the vote in the 2014 European election under its previous name, Front National, and that current polling suggested a weaker performance than that. In the Netherlands, the eurosceptic Forum for Democracy party, led by Thierry Baudet, was projected to make gains following a strong showing in earlier Dutch provincial elections, with analysts suggesting it could become the largest individual Dutch party in the European election and a significant addition to the European Conservatives and Reformists group in the new Parliament. Projections also pointed to a tight race between the centre-right European People's Party and centre-left Socialists and Democrats group for the largest overall bloc in the new Parliament, a contest complicated by uncertainty over whether Hungary's ruling Fidesz party would remain aligned with the centre-right group following a dispute over its leadership candidate. The article also noted the United Kingdom's confirmed participation in the European election despite Brexit, following a wave of support for explicitly pro-EU parties in recent UK local elections.

title: Le Pen tops EU election in France, dealing setback to Macron
text: Marine Le Pen's National Rally party appeared set to finish first in France's European Parliament election on election night, according to exit polls, narrowly ahead of President Emmanuel Macron's centrist alliance, in what was described as a blow to the pro-EU French leader. Exit polls put National Rally at roughly 24 percent against Macron's alliance at around 22.5 to 23 percent. The head of National Rally's campaign called the result a failure for Macron's ruling party and framed the outcome as a rejection of the president's pro-business, pro-EU agenda. Le Pen called for Macron to dissolve parliament and hold new elections, a proposal his government rejected. Despite the narrow win, the result was described as a mixed outcome for Le Pen, whose party's projected vote share was lower than the 24.9 percent it won under its previous name in the 2014 European election. A Macron aide described the exit poll results as respectable, while the French prime minister said the outcome confirmed a broader realignment of French politics that began with Macron's 2017 presidential win. The article also noted strong gains for the French Green party, continuing a trend seen elsewhere in the EU, while France's traditional centre-right Republicans and the Socialist-led centre-left alliance both continued to struggle, polling at around 8 percent and roughly 6.5 percent respectively. Turnout was reported as significantly higher than in the previous two European elections, rising to the low 50s percent compared to 35 percent in 2014 and 33 percent in 2009.

title: The far right's gains across the 2019 European Parliament election
text: Following the 2019 European Parliament election, commentary on the rise of far-right and nationalist parties noted that while some forces, including Italy's Lega, the Brexit Party in the UK, and France's National Rally (formerly Front National) under Marine Le Pen, achieved strong results, the overall picture across the EU was more mixed than a single narrative of far-right triumph suggested. The piece argued that nationalist and populist parties remained fragmented across multiple political groupings in the new European Parliament, limiting their practical influence despite individual victories in large economies such as the UK, France and Italy. It noted that some of the far right's success reflected a shift away from social-media-driven, insurgent campaigning toward more established, party-machine-style politics, with previously fringe figures and organizations increasingly normalized within mainstream political discourse. The analysis also observed that Steve Bannon's transatlantic organizing efforts on behalf of European far-right parties appeared to have had limited practical effect, with the one party that explicitly aligned with his network winning a small vote share and no seats. The piece further noted that in Germany, the far-right Alternative for Germany's vote share had actually declined slightly compared to its 2017 national election result, even as its overall position consolidated. The analysis concluded that across several European countries, centrist parties had been able to grow their own vote share by explicitly positioning themselves as the strongest available challenge to far-right gains, complicating any simple story of uniform far-right ascendance.

title: Dutch parties react to European Parliament election results
text: Final results of the European Parliament election in the Netherlands showed the Labour party (PvdA) winning the most seats among Dutch parties, becoming the largest Dutch party in the European Parliament for the first time since 1984, according to PvdA leader Lodewijk Asscher, who called the result very spectacular. Labour's lead candidate, Frans Timmermans, said he still intended to pursue the European Commission presidency despite his party's broader losses across the EU, arguing that a progressive, socially-minded coalition remained possible in the more fragmented new Parliament. The eurosceptic Forum for Democracy (FvD), led by Thierry Baudet, went from zero seats to three, a result its lead candidate Derk-Jan Eppink described as a major win that had been overshadowed in coverage by Labour's surprise victory. The Socialist Party (SP) lost its only seat, which its leadership called a hard blow. D66, led at the European level by Sophie in 't Veld, saw its seat count fall from four to two, though in 't Veld argued the result still left room for her party's liberal group to grow more influential in a more fragmented Parliament. The Christian Democratic Appeal (CDA), represented by Esther de Lange, performed roughly in line with expectations. GroenLinks, led by Jesse Klaver, welcomed strong results for Green parties across multiple EU countries, with Klaver highlighting particularly high support for Green parties among younger voters. Across the EU, the two largest political groupings, the centre-right European People's Party and centre-left Socialists and Democrats, both lost their combined majority in the European Parliament for the first time, while Green and liberal groupings gained seats overall.
